tree.title: Vector Projection
tree.summary: This tutorial explains vector projection, providing the mathematical formula and interactive experiments to demonstrate how to project one vector onto another and understand its geometric significance.

children.title: 1. Interactive Playground
children.summary: This section describes an interactive tool where users can manipulate vector endpoints by dragging or editing values to visualize the projection of vector a onto vector b, which is displayed in green.

children.title: Interactive Vector Projection Playground
children.summary: An interactive tool where users can manipulate vector endpoints and observe the projection of one vector onto another.

children.title: 2. What is Vector Projection?
children.summary: Vector projection finds the "shadow" or component of one vector that lies along the direction of another vector, essentially measuring how much of the first vector points in the same direction as the second.

children.title: Streetlight Shadow Analogy for Vector Projection
children.summary: Vector projection is introduced through the analogy of a shadow cast by a streetlight onto the ground.

children.title: Definition and Purpose of Vector Projection
children.summary: Vector projection finds the component of one vector that lies along the direction of another vector.

children.title: 3. The Formula
children.summary: The projection of vector a onto vector b is calculated using the formula proj_b(a) = ((a · b) / |b|²) × b, which measures how much of vector a points in the same direction as vector b.

children.title: Pythagorean Connection and Dot Product Self-Multiplication
children.summary: Explains how the dot product formula relates to the Pythagorean theorem when a vector is dotted with itself versus when two different vectors are used.

children.title: Two Different Vectors and Right Triangle Geometry
children.summary: Demonstrates how dot products work with two different vectors using right triangle visualization and explains the geometric relationship between separate vector triangles.

children.title: Why Multiplication Instead of Addition in Dot Products
children.summary: Explains why multiplication captures directional agreement between vectors while addition would lose crucial alignment information.

children.title: Fourier Transform Analogy and Signal Resonance
children.summary: Connects dot product directional agreement to Fourier transforms, showing how both measure resonance or alignment between different mathematical objects.

children.title: Scalar Result and Vector-to-Number Conversion
children.summary: Explains how the dot product divided by the squared length produces a scalar that measures how much of one vector lies along another's direction.

children.title: 4. Alternative Form: Unit Vectors
children.summary: When vector **b** is normalized to a unit vector **b̂**, the projection formula simplifies to proj_b(a) = (a · b̂) × b̂, where the dot product directly gives the scalar projection length.

children.title: Understanding Scalar and Vector Projections
children.summary: The dot product (a · b̂) gives the scalar projection, which becomes a vector when multiplied by the unit vector b̂.

children.title: 5. Why Does This Matter?
children.summary: Vector projection is widely used across various technical fields and applications.

children.title: 6. Experiments to Try
children.summary: This section suggests experimenting with the visualization to discover that a vector projection's magnitude depends only on the angle between the vectors, not on the length of the vector being projected onto.

children.title: Interactive Visualization Experiments Introduction
children.summary: Introduction to hands-on experiments using the projection visualization tool.

children.title: Projection Magnitude Independence Hint
children.summary: Key insight that projection magnitude depends only on vector angle, not on the length of vector b.

children.title: Section 7
children.summary: The scalar obtained by dividing the dot product a·b by the squared magnitude of vector b tells us exactly how far to travel along vector b's direction to reach the projection point, serving as a coordinate that specifies the projection's location on vector b.

children.title: The Scalar as Heart of Vector Projection
children.summary: The scalar from the projection formula tells us exactly how far along vector b the projection extends.

children.title: Numerical Example of Scalar Calculation
children.summary: A concrete example showing how to calculate the projection scalar using a·b divided by |b|².

children.title: GPS Highway Analogy for Understanding Scalars
children.summary: The scalar is like a GPS mile marker that tells you exactly how far along a highway to travel.

children.title: Converting Scalar to Complete Projection Vector
children.summary: To get the actual projection vector, multiply the scalar by b's unit vector, combining magnitude and direction.

children.title: Section 8
children.summary: This section explains how vector projection can be extended from projecting onto a line (1D) to projecting onto a plane (2D) by using two basis vectors that define the plane and combining their components, similar to dropping a shadow onto a tilted surface in 3D space.

children.title: 3D Vector Projection onto Plane Extension
children.summary: Confirmation that projecting 3D vectors onto planes would be an excellent natural extension of the current material.

children.title: Shadow Analogy and Plane Spanning Concept
children.summary: Explains projection onto a plane using the analogy of dropping a shadow onto tilted paper, where the plane is spanned by two basis vectors.

children.title: Mathematical Process for Plane Projection
children.summary: Describes how the projection process uses dot products to find alignment with each plane vector and combines those components.

children.title: Dimensional Scaling and Visualization Benefits
children.summary: Explains how this extension demonstrates projection scaling from 1D to 2D and provides visualization opportunities for understanding geometric concepts.
